english_head: But really, Theaetetus, our talk has been badly tainted with unclearness all along; for we have said over and over again we know and we do not know and we have knowledge and we have no knowledge, as if we could understand each other, while we were still ignorant of knowledge; and at this very moment, if you please, we have again used the terms be ignorant and understand, as though we had any right to use them if we are deprived of knowledge. But how will you converse, Socrates, if you refrain fr

greek_head: Οὐδένα ὤν γε ὃς εἰμί εἰ μέντοι ἦ ἀντιλογικός οἷος ἀνὴρ εἰ καὶ νῦν παρῆν τούτων τ' ἂν ἔφη ἀπέχεσθαι καὶ
greek_tokens: oudeis eimi ge os eimi ei mentoi eimi antilogikos oios anhr ei kai nun pareimi outos su an fhmi apexw kai egw sfodra an o egw legos epiplhssw epei oun eimi faulos boulomai tolmaw eipon oios eimi o efisthmi fainw gar egw prourgou tis an gignomai tolma toinun nh dios outos de mh apexw su eimi polus suggnwmh akouw oun o nun legw o efisthmi isos ou mentoi en ge o pareimi mnhmoneuw
english_head: Not at all, being the man I am; but I might if I were a real reasoner; if such a man were present at this moment he would tell us to refrain from these terms, and would criticize my talk scathingly. But since we are poor creatures, shall I venture to say what the nature of knowing is? For it seems to me that would be of some advantage. Venture it then, by Zeus. You shall have full pardon for not refraining from those terms. Have you heard what they say nowadays that knowing is? Perhaps; however,

greek_head: Ἐπιστήμης που ἕξιν φασὶν αὐτὸ εἶναι Ἀληθῆ
greek_tokens: episthmh pou ecis fhmi autos eimi alhqhs egw toinun mikros metaqew kai eipon episthmh ktaomai tis oun dh fhmi outos ekeinos diaferw isos men oudeis o de oun dokew aekwn sundokimazw ean ge oios su eimi ou toinun egw tautos fainw o ktaomai o exw oios imation priamai tis kai egkraths eimi mh fora exw men ou an autos autos ktaomai ge meis fhmi orqos ge
english_head: They say it is having knowledge. True. Let us make a slight change and say possessing knowledge. Why, how will you claim that the one differs from the other? Perhaps it doesn’t; but first hear how it seems to me to differ, and then help me to test my view. I will if I can. Well, then, having does not seem to me the same as possessing. For instance, if a man bought a cloak and had it under his control, but did not wear it, we should certainly say, not that he had it, but that he possessed it. And

greek_head: Ὅρα δὴ καὶ ἐπιστήμην εἰ δυνατὸν οὕτω κεκτημένον μὴ ἔχειν ἀλλ' ὥσπερ εἴ τις ὄρνιθας ἀγρίας περιστερὰς ἤ
greek_tokens: oraw dh kai episthmh ei dunatos outws ktaomai mh exw alla wsper ei tis ornis agrios peristera h tis allos qhraw eoika kataskeuazw peristerewn trefw tropos men gar an pou tis fhmi autos autos aei exw ostis dh ktaomai eimi gar nai tropos de ge allos oudeis exw alla dunamis men autos peri autos paragignomai epei en oikeios peribolos upoxeirios poiew lambanw kai exw epeidan
english_head: Now see whether it is possible in the same way for one who possesses knowledge not to have it, as, for instance, if a man should catch wild birds—pigeons or the like—and should arrange an aviary at home and keep them in it, we might in a way assert that he always has them because he possesses them, might we not? Yes. And yet in another way that he has none of them, but that he has acquired power over them, since he has brought them under his control in his own enclosure,

greek_head: βούληται θηρευσαμένῳ ἣν ἂν ἀεὶ ἐθέλῃ καὶ πάλιν ἀφιέναι καὶ τοῦτο ἐξεῖναι ποιεῖν ὁποσάκις ἂν δοκῇ αὐτῷ
greek_tokens: boulomai qhreuw os an aei eqelw kai palin afihmi kai outos ecesti poiew oposakis an dokazw autos eimi outos palin dh wsper en o prosqen khrinos tis en o yuxh kataskeuazw ou oida ostis plasma nun au en ekastos yuxw poiew peristerewn tis pantodaphs ornis o men kata agelh eimi xwris o allos o de kata oligos enioi de monos dia pas oph an tugxanw petomai
english_head: to take them and hold them whenever he likes, by catching whichever bird he pleases, and to let them go again; and he can do this as often as be sees fit. That is true. Once more, then, just as a while ago we contrived some sort of a waxen figment in the soul, so now let us make in each soul an aviary stocked with all sorts of birds, some in flocks apart from the rest, others in small groups, and some solitary, flying hither and thither among them all.

greek_head: Πεποιήσθω δή ἀλλὰ τί τοὐντεῦθεν Παιδίων μὲν ὄντων φάναι χρὴ εἶναι τοῦτο τὸ ἀγγεῖον
greek_tokens: poiew dh alla tis enteuqen paidion men eimi fainw xrh eimi outos o aggeion kenos anti de o ornis episthmh noew os de an episthmh ktaomai kaqeircis eis o peribolos fainw autos manqanw h euriskw o pragma e eimi outos o episthmh kai o efisthmi outos eimi eimi
english_head: Consider it done. What next? We must assume that while we are children this receptacle is empty, and we must understand that the birds represent the varieties of knowledge. And whatsoever kind of knowledge a person acquires and shuts up in the enclosure, we must say that he has learned or discovered the thing of which this is the knowledge, and that just this is knowing. So be it.

greek_head: Τὸ τοίνυν πάλιν ἣν ἂν βούληται τῶν ἐπιστημῶν θηρεύειν καὶ λαβόντα ἴσχειν καὶ αὖθις ἀφιέναι σκόπει τίνων
greek_tokens: o toinun palin os an boulomai o episthmh qhreuw kai lambanw isxw kai auqis afihmi skopaw tinw deomai onoma eite o autos os o proteros oste ktaomai eite eteros maqhsis de enqende safhs tis legos ariqmhtikos men gar legw texnh nai outos dh upolambanw qhra episthmh artios te kai perissos pas upolambanw outos dh oimh o texnh autos te upoxeirios o
english_head: Consider then what expressions are needed for the process of recapturing and taking and holding and letting go again whichever he please of the kinds of knowledge, whether they are the same expressions as those needed for the original acquisition, or others. But you will understand better by an illustration. You admit that there is an art of arithmetic? Yes. Now suppose this to be a hunt after the kinds of knowledge, or sciences, of all odd and even numbers. I do so. Now it is by this art, I ima

greek_head: ἐπιστήμας τῶν ἀριθμῶν ἔχει καὶ ἄλλῳ παραδίδωσιν ὁ παραδιδούς
greek_tokens: episthmh o ariqmew exis kai allos paradidwmi o paradidwmi nai kai kalew ge paradidwmi men didaskw paralambanw de manqanw exw de dh o ktaomai en o peristerewn ekeinos efisthmi panu men oun o de dh enteuqen hdh prosexw o new ariqmhtikos gar eimi teleios allos tis pas ariqmos efisthmi pas gar ariqmew eimi autos en o yuxw episthmh tis meis
english_head: the sciences of numbers under his own control and also that any man who transmits them to another does this. Yes. And we say that when anyone transmits them he teaches, and when anyone receives them he learns, and when anyone, by having acquired them, has them in that aviary of ours, he knows them. Certainly. Now pay attention to what follows from this. Does not the perfect arithmetician understand all numbers; for he has the sciences of all numbers in his mind? To be sure.

greek_head: Ἦ οὖν ὁ τοιοῦτος ἀριθμοῖ ἄν ποτέ τι ἢ αὐτὸς πρὸς αὑτὸν αὐτὰ ἢ ἄλλο τι τῶν ἔξω ὅσα ἔχει ἀριθμόν
greek_tokens: eimi oun o toioutos ariqmew an pote tis h autos pros eautou autos h allos tis o ecw osos exis ariqmos pws gar ou o de ariqmew ge ou allos tis tiqhmi o skopaw posos tis ariqmos tugxanw eimi outws o ara efisthmi skopaw fainw ws ou eidoi os omologew apas ariqmos oida akouw gar pou o toioutos amfisbhthsis egw
english_head: Then would such a man ever count anything—either any abstract numbers in his head, or any such external objects as possess number? Of course, But we shall affirm that counting is the same thing as considering how great any number in question is. We shall. Then he who by our previous admission knows all number is found to be considering that which he knows as if he did not know it. You have doubtless heard of such ambiguities. Yes, I have. Continuing, then, our comparison with the acquisition

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ἡμεῖς ἀπεικάζοντες τῇ τῶν περιστερῶν κτήσει τε καὶ θήρᾳ ἐροῦμεν ὅτι διττὴ ἦν ἡ θήρα ἡ μὲν πρὶν ἐκτῆσθαι
greek_tokens: oukoun egw apeikazw o o peristera ktaomai te kai qhra erew ostis dissos eimi o qhra o men prin ktaomai o ktaomai eneka o de ktaomai o lambanw kai exw en o xeir o pala ktaomai outws de kai os pala episthmh eimi autos manqanw kai epistamai autos palin eimi katamanqanw tautos outos analambanw o episthmh ekastos kai isxw os ktaomai men pala proxeiron de ou exw o dianoia alhqhs
english_head: and hunting of the pigeons, we shall say that the hunting is of two kinds, one before the acquisition for the sake of possessing, the other carried on by the possessor for the sake of taking and holding in his hands what he had acquired long before. And just so when a man long since by learning came to possess knowledge of certain things, and knew them, he may have these very things afresh by taking up again the knowledge of each of them separately and holding it—the knowledge which he had acqui

greek_head: Τοῦτο δὴ ἄρτι ἠρώτων ὅπως χρὴ τοῖς ὀνόμασι χρώμενον λέγειν περὶ αὐτῶν ὅταν ἀριθμήσων ἴῃ ὁ ἀριθμητικὸς
greek_tokens: outos dh arti erwtaw opws xrh o onoma xraomai legw peri autos otan ariqmew eimi o ariqmhtikos h tis anagignwskw o grammatikos ws efisthmi ara en o toioutos palin erxomai manqanw para eautou o efisthmi alla atopos eimi swkraths alla o ou efisthmi fhmi autos anagignwskw
english_head: This, then, was my question just now: How should we express ourselves in speaking about them when an arithmetician undertakes to count or a man of letters to read something? In such a case shall we say that although he knows he sets himself to learn again from himself that which he knows? But that is extraordinary, Socrates. But shall we say that he is going to read or count that which he does not know, when we have granted that he knows all letters and all numbers?

greek_head: καὶ ἀριθμήσειν δεδωκότες αὐτῷ πάντα μὲν γράμματα πάντα δὲ ἀριθμὸν ἐπίστασθαι
greek_tokens: kai ariqmew didwmi autos pas men gramma pas de ariqmos efisthmi alla kai outos alogos boulomai oun legw ostis o men onoma oudeis egw melos oph tis xairw elkw o efisthmi kai manqanw epei de orizw eteros men tis o ktaomai o episthmh eteros de o exw o men tis ktaomai mh ktaomai adunatos fhmi eimi wste oudepote sumbainw o tis oida mh oida yeudhs mentoi doca oios su eimi peri autos lambanw
english_head: But that too is absurd. Shall we then say that words are nothing to us, if it amuses anyone to drag the expressions know and learn one way and another, but since we set up the distinction that it is one thing to possess knowledge and another thing to have it, we affirm that it is impossible not to possess what one possesses, so that it never happens that a man does not know that which he knows, but that it is possible to conceive a false opinion about it?

greek_head: μὴ γὰρ ἔχειν τὴν ἐπιστήμην τούτου οἷόν τε ἀλλ' ἑτέραν ἀντ' ἐκείνης ὅταν θηρεύων τινά πού ποτ' ἐπιστήμην διαπετομένων

english_head: For it is possible to have not the knowledge of this thing, but some other knowledge instead, when in hunting for some one kind of knowledge, as the various kinds fly about, he makes a mistake and catches one instead of another; so in one example he thought eleven was twelve, because he caught the knowledge of twelve, which was within him, instead of that of eleven, caught a ringdove, as it were, instead of a pigeon. Yes, that is reasonable. But when he catches the knowledge he intends to catch,

greek_head: ψευδῆ δόξαν καὶ ὧν ἐν τοῖς πρόσθεν ἐδυσχεραίνομεν οὐδὲν ἐμποδὼν γίγνεσθαι ἴσως οὖν μοι συμφήσεις ἢ πῶς
greek_tokens: yeudhs doca kai os en o prosqen dusxerainw oudeis empodwn gignomai isos oun egw sumfhmi h pws poihsis outws kai gar o men o efisthmi mh efisthmi apallassw o gar ktaomai mh ktaomai oudamos eti sumbainw oute yeudw tis oute mh deinos mentoi paqos allos parafainw egw dokew o poios ei o o episthmh metallagh yeudhs gignomai pote doca pws dh
english_head: which formerly annoyed us interferes? Perhaps you will agree to this; or what will you do? I will agree. Yes, for we have got rid of our difficulty about men not knowing that which they know; for we no longer find ourselves not possessing that which we possess, whether we are deceived about anything or not. However, another more dreadful disaster seems to be coming in sight. What disaster? If the interchange of kinds of knowledge should ever turn out to be false opinion. How so?

greek_head: Πρῶτον μὲν τό τινος ἔχοντα ἐπιστήμην τοῦτο αὐτὸ ἀγνοεῖν μὴ ἀγνωμοσύνῃ ἀλλὰ τῇ ἑαυτοῦ ἐπιστήμῃ ἔπειτα
greek_tokens: proteros men o tis exw episthmh outos autos agnoew mh agnwmosunh alla o eautou episthmh epeita eteros au outos docazw o de eteros outos pws ou polus alogia episthmh paragignomai gignwskw men o yuxh mhdeis agnoew de pas ek gar outos o logos kwluw oudeis kai agnoia paragignomai gignwskw tis poiew kai tufloths eidon eiper kai episthmh agnoew pote tis poihsis
english_head: Is it not the height of absurdity, in the first place for one who has knowledge of something to be ignorant of this very thing, not through ignorance but through his knowledge; secondly, for him to be of opinion that this thing is something else and something else is this thing—for the soul, when knowledge has come to it, to know nothing and be ignorant of all things? For by this argument there is nothing to prevent ignorance from coming to us and making us know something and blindness from maki

greek_head: Ἴσως γάρ ὦ Σώκρατες οὐ καλῶς τὰς ὄρνιθας ἐτίθεμεν ἐπιστήμας μόνον τιθέντες ἔδει δὲ καὶ ἀνεπιστημοσύνας
greek_tokens: isos gar eimi swkraths ou kalos o ornis tiqhmi episthmh monos tiqhmi dew de kai anepisthmosunh tiqhmi omos sundiapetamai en o yuxw kai o qhreuw tote men episthmh lambanw tote de anepisthmosunh o autos peri yeudhs men docazw o anepisthmosunh alhqhs de o episthmh ou radios ge eimi qeaithtos mh epainew su o mentoi eipon palin episkeptomai eimi men gar ws legw o de dh o
english_head: Perhaps, Socrates, we were not right in making the birds represent kinds of knowledge only, but we ought to have imagined kinds of ignorance also flying about in the soul with the others; then the hunter would catch sometimes knowledge and sometimes ignorance of the same thing, and through the ignorance he would have false, but through the knowledge true opinion. It is not easy, Theaetetus, to refrain from praising you. However, examine your suggestion once more. Let it be as you say:

greek_head: ἀνεπιστημοσύνην λαβὼν ψευδῆ μέν φῄς δοξάσει ἦ γάρ Ναί
greek_tokens: anepisthmosunh lambanw yeudhs men fhmi docasis eimi gar nai ou dhpou kai ageomai ge yeudhs docazw pws gar alla alhqhs ge kai ws eidoi diakeimai peri os yeudw tis meis episthmh ara oiomai qhreuw exw alla ou anepisthmosunh dhlos oukoun makran perierxomai palin epi o proteros pareimi aporia o gar elegktikos ekeinos gelaw fhmi
english_head: the man who catches the ignorance will, you say, have false opinion. Is that it? Yes. But surely he will not also think that he has false opinion. Certainly not. No, but true opinion, and will have the attitude of knowing that about which he is deceived. Of course. Hence he will fancy that he has caught, and has, knowledge, not ignorance. Evidently. Then, after our long wanderings, we have come round again to our first difficulty. For the real reasoner

greek_head: Πότερον ὦ βέλτιστοι ἀμφοτέρας τις εἰδώς ἐπιστήμην τε καὶ ἀνεπιστημοσύνην ἣν οἶδεν ἑτέραν αὐτὴν οἴεταί τινα
greek_tokens: poteros eimi beltistos amfoteros tis eidoi episthmh te kai anepisthmosunh os oida eteros autos oiomai tis eimi os oida h oudeteros autos eidoi os mh oida docazw eteros os ou oida h o men eidoi o de ou os oida os mh oida h os mh oida os oida hgeomai h palin au egw erew ostis o episthmh kai anepisthmosunh eimi au episthmh os o ktaomai en eteros tis geloios
english_head: will laugh and say, Most excellent Sirs, does a man who knows both knowledge and ignorance think that one of them, which he knows, is another thing which he knows; or, knowing neither of them, is he of opinion that one, which he does not know, is another thing which he does not know; or, knowing one and not the other, does he think that the one he does not know is the one he knows; or that the one he knows is the one he does not know? Or will you go on and tell me that there are kinds of knowled

greek_head: περιστερεῶσιν ἢ κηρίνοις πλάσμασι καθείρξας ἕωσπερ ἂν κεκτῆται ἐπίσταται καὶ ἐὰν μὴ προχείρους ἔχῃ ἐν τῇ ψυχῇ
greek_tokens: peristerewn h khrinos plasma kateirgw ewsper an ktaomai efisthmi kai ean mh proxeiros exis en o yuxw kai outws dh anagkazw eis tautos peritrexw muriakis oudeis pleos poiew tis pros outos eimi qeaithtos apokrinw alla ma diw eimi swkraths egw ou exw tis xrh legw ara oun egw eimi pais kalos o logos epiplhssw kai endeiknumi ostis ou orqos yeudhs doca proteros zhtew
english_head: or waxen figments, knows them, so long as he possesses them, even if he has them not at hand in his soul? And in this fashion are you going to be compelled to trot about endlessly in the same circle without making any progress? What shall we reply to this, Theaetetus? By Zeus, Socrates, I don’t know what to say. Then, my boy, is the argument right in rebuking us and in pointing out that we were wrong to abandon knowledge and seek first for false opinion?

greek_head: ἐπιστήμης ἐκείνην ἀφέντες τὸ δ' ἐστὶν ἀδύνατον γνῶναι πρὶν ἄν τις ἐπιστήμην ἱκανῶς λάβῃ τί ποτ' ἐστίν
greek_tokens: episthmh ekeinos afihmi o de eimi adunatos gignwskw prin an tis episthmh ikanos lambanw tis pote eimi anagkh eimi swkraths en o pareimi ws legw oiomai tis oun tis eraomai palin ek arxh episthmh ou gar pou aperaw ge pw hkistos ean mh su ge apagoreuw legos dh tis an autos malista eipon hkistos an egw autos enantioomai
english_head: It is impossible to know the latter until we have adequately comprehended the nature of knowledge. As the case now stands, Socrates, we cannot help thinking as you say. To begin, then, at the beginning once more, what shall we say knowledge is? For surely we are not going to give it up yet, are we? Not by any means, unless, that is, you give it up. Tell us, then, what definition will make us contradict ourselves least.

greek_head: Ὅπερ ἐπεχειροῦμεν ὦ Σώκρατες ἐν τῷ πρόσθεν οὐ γὰρ ἔχω ἔγωγε ἄλλο οὐδέν
greek_tokens: osper epixeirew eimi swkraths en o prosqen ou gar exw egw allos oudeis o poios o alhqhs doca episthmh eimi anamarthtos ge pou eimi o docazw alhqhs kai o upo autos gignomai pas kalos kai agaqos gignomai o o potamos kaqhgeomai eimi qeaithtos fhmi ara deiknumi autos kai outos ean eimi ereunaw taxa an
english_head: The one we tried before, Socrates; at any rate, I have nothing else to offer. What one? That knowledge is true opinion; for true opinion is surely free from error and all its results are fine and good. The man who was leading the way through the river, Theaetetus, said: The result itself will show; and so in this matter, if we go on with our search, perhaps the thing will turn up in our path and of itself reveal the object of our search;

greek_head: ἐμπόδιον γενόμενον αὐτὸ φήνειεν τὸ ζητούμενον μένουσι δὲ δῆλον οὐδέν
greek_tokens: empodios gignomai autos fainw o zhtew menw de dhlos oudeis orqos legw alla eimi ge kai skopaw oukoun outos ge braxus skeyis texnh gar su oloc shmainw mh eimi episthmh autos pws dh kai tis outos o o megas eis sofia os dh kalew rhtwr te kai dikanikos outos gar pou o eautou texnh peiqw ou didaskw alla docazw poiew o an boulomai h su oiomai deinos tis outws didaskalos eimi
english_head: but if we stay still, we shall discover nothing. You are right; let us go on with our investigation. Well, then, this at least calls for slight investigation; for you have a whole profession which declares that true opinion is not knowledge. How so? What profession is it? The profession of those who are greatest in wisdom, who are called orators and lawyers; for they persuade men by the art which they possess, not teaching them, but making them have whatever opinion they like. Or do you think th

greek_head: ὥστε οἷς μὴ παρεγένοντό τινες ἀποστερουμένοις χρήματα ἤ τι ἄλλο βιαζομένοις τούτοις δύνασθαι πρὸς ὕδωρ σμικρὸν

english_head: satisfactorily to teach the judges the truth about what happened to people who have been robbed of their money or have suffered other acts of violence, when there were no eyewitnesses? I certainly do not think so; but I think they can persuade them. And persuading them is making them have an opinion, is it not? Of course. Then when judges are justly persuaded about matters which one can know only by having seen them and in no other way, in such a case, judging of them from hearsay, having acquir

greek_head: ἀκοῆς κρίνοντες ἀληθῆ δόξαν λαβόντες ἄνευ ἐπιστήμης ἔκριναν ὀρθὰ πεισθέντες εἴπερ εὖ ἐδίκασαν
greek_tokens: akoh krinw alhqhs doca lambanw aneu episthmh krinw orqos peiqw eiper eu dikazw pantapasi men oun ou an eimi filos ei ge tautos eimi doca te alhqhs kai dikasthrion kai episthmh orqos pote an dikasths akros docazw aneu episthmh nun de eoika allos tis ekateros eimi o ge egw eimi swkraths eipon tis aekwn epilanqanomai nun de ennoew fhmi de o men meta logos
english_head: they have judged without knowledge, though they are rightly persuaded, if the judgement they have passed is correct, have they not? Certainly. But, my friend, if true opinion and knowledge were the same thing in law courts, the best of judges could never have true opinion without knowledge; in fact, however, it appears that the two are different. Oh yes, I remember now, Socrates, having heard someone make the distinction, but I had forgotten it. He said that knowledge was true opinion accompanie

english_head: but that unreasoning true opinion was outside of the sphere of knowledge; and matters of which there is not a rational explanation are unknowable—yes, that is what he called them—and those of which there is are knowable. I am glad you mentioned that. But tell us how he distinguished between the knowable and the unknowable, that we may see whether the accounts that you and I have heard agree. But I do not know whether I can think it out; but if someone else were to make the statement of it, I thi

greek_head: ἀκούειν τινῶν ὅτι τὰ μὲν πρῶτα οἱονπερεὶ στοιχεῖα ἐξ ὧν ἡμεῖς τε συγκείμεθα καὶ τἆλλα λόγον οὐκ ἔχοι αὐτὸ γὰρ

english_head: used to imagine that I heard certain persons say that the primary elements of which we and all else are composed admit of no rational explanation; for each alone by itself can only be named, and no qualification can be added, neither that it is nor that it is not,

greek_head: ἂν οὐσίαν ἢ μὴ οὐσίαν αὐτῷ προστίθεσθαι δεῖν δὲ οὐδὲν προσφέρειν εἴπερ αὐτὸ ἐκεῖνο μόνον τις ἐρεῖ ἐπεὶ οὐδὲ

english_head: for that would at once be adding to it existence or non-existence, whereas we must add nothing to it, if we are to speak of that itself alone. Indeed, not even itself or that or each or alone or this or anything else of the sort, of which there are many, must be added; for these are prevalent terms which are added to all things indiscriminately and are different from the things to which they are added; but if it were possible to explain an element, and it admitted of a rational explanation of it

greek_head: ὁτιοῦν τῶν πρώτων ῥηθῆναι λόγῳ οὐ γὰρ εἶναι αὐτῷ ἀλλ' ἢ ὀνομάζεσθαι μόνον ὄνομα γὰρ μόνον ἔχειν τὰ δὲ ἐκ

english_head: they can only be named, for they have only a name; but the things composed of these are themselves complex, and so their names are complex and form a rational explanation; for the combination of names is the essence of reasoning. Thus the elements are not objects of reason or of knowledge, but only of perception, whereas the combinations of them are objects of knowledge and expression and true opinion. When therefore a man acquires without reasoning the true opinion about anything,

english_head: his mind has the truth about it, but has no knowledge; for he who cannot give and receive a rational explanation of a thing is without knowledge of it; but when he has acquired also a rational explanation he may possibly have become all that I have said and may now be perfect in knowledge. Is that the version of the dream you have heard, or is it different? That was it exactly. Are you satisfied, then, and do you state it in this way, that true opinion accompanied by reason is knowledge? Precise

english_head: Can it be, Theaetetus, that we now, in this casual manner, have found out on this day what many wise men have long been seeking and have grown grey in the search? I, at any rate, Socrates, think our present statement is good. Probably this particular statement is so; for what knowledge could there still be apart from reason and right opinion? One point, however, in what has been said is unsatisfactory to me. What point? Just that which seems to be the cleverest; the assertion that the elements a

greek_tokens: stoixeion agnwstos o de o sullabh genos gnwstos oukoun orqos isteon dh wsper gar omhros exw o logos o paradeigma os xraomai eipon pas outos poios dh o o gramma stoixeion te kai sullabh h oiomai allote poi blepw outos eipon o eipon o legw ou alla eis outos
english_head: is knowable. Is that not right? We are sure to find out, for we have as hostages the examples which he who said all this used in his argument. What examples? The elements in writing, the letters of the alphabet, and their combinations, the syllables ; or do you think the author of the statements we are discussing had something else in view? No; those are what he had in view.

greek_head: Βασανίζωμεν δὴ αὐτὰ ἀναλαμβάνοντες μᾶλλον δὲ ἡμᾶς αὐτούς οὕτως ἢ οὐχ οὕτως γράμματα ἐμάθομεν φέρε
greek_tokens: basanizw dh autos analambanw mallon de egw autos outws h ou outws gramma manqanw ferw proteros ara o men sullabh logos exw o de stoixeion alogos isos panu men oun kai egw fainw swkraths goun ei tis eromai o proteros sullabh outwsi eimi qeaithtos legos tis eimi sw tis apokrinw ostis sigma kai eimi oukoun outos exis logos o sullabh egw
english_head: Let us, then, take them up and examine them, or rather, let us examine ourselves and see whether it was in accordance with this theory, or not, that we learned letters. First then, the syllables have a rational explanation, but the letters have not? I suppose so. I think so, too, decidedly. Now if anyone should ask about the first syllable of Socrates; Theaetetus, tell me, what is SO? What would you reply? I should say S and O. This, then, is your explanation of the syllable? Yes.

greek_head: Ἴθι δή οὕτως εἰπὲ καὶ τὸν τοῦ σῖγμα λόγον Καὶ πῶς τοῦ στοιχείου τις ἐρεῖ στοιχεῖα καὶ
greek_tokens: eimi dh outws eipon kai o o sigma logos kai pws o stoixeion tis eraomai stoixeion kai gar dh eimi swkraths o te sigma o afwnos eimi yofos tis monos oios surizw o glwssa o de au bhta oute fwnh oute yofos oude o pleistos stoixeion wste panu eu exis o legw autos alogos os ge o enarghs autos o epta fwnh monos exis logos de oude ostis outos men ara eimi etairos katorqow peri episthmh fainw
english_head: Come now, in the same manner give me the explanation of the S. How can one give any elements of an element? For really, Socrates, the S is a voiceless letter, a mere noise, as of the tongue hissing; B again has neither voice nor noise, nor have most of the other letters; and so it is quite right to say that they have no explanation, seeing that the most distinct of them, the seven vowels, have only voice, but no explanation whatsoever. In this point, then, my friend, it would seem that we have r

greek_head: Τί δέ τὸ μὴ γνωστὸν εἶναι τὸ στοιχεῖον ἀλλὰ τὴν συλλαβὴν ἆρ' ὀρθῶς ἀποδεδείγμεθα
greek_tokens: tis de o mh gnwstos eimi o stoixeion alla o sullabh ara orqos apodeiknumi eoika ge ferw dh o sullabh poteros legw o amfoteros stoixeion kai ean plew eimi h duo o pas h eis tis idea gignomai suntiqhmi autos o apas egw dokew oraw dh epi duo sigma kai eimi amfoteros eimi o proteros sullabh o egw onoma allos tis o gignwskw autos o amfoteros gignwskw
english_head: But have we been right in laying down the principle that whereas the letter is unknowable, yet the syllable is knowable? Probably. Well then, shall we say that the syllable is the two letters, or, if there be more than two, all of them, or is it a single concept that has arisen from their combination? I think we mean all the letters it contains. Now take the case of two, S and O. The two together are the first syllable of my name. He who knows it knows the two letters, does he not?

greek_head: Τί μήν Τὸ σῖγμα καὶ τὸ ὦ ἄρα γιγνώσκει
greek_tokens: tis meis o sigma kai o eimi ara gignwskw nai tis de ekateros ara agnoew kai oudeteros eidoi amfoteros gignwskw alla deinos kai alogos eimi swkraths alla mentoi ei ge anagkh ekateros gignwskw eiper amfoteros tis gignwskw progignwskw o stoixeion apas anagkh o mellw pote gignwskw sullabh kai outws egw o kalos logos apodidraskw oixomai
english_head: Of course. He knows, that is, the S and the O. Yes. How is that? He is ignorant of each, and knowing neither of them he knows them both? That is monstrous and absurd, Socrates. And yet if a knowledge of each letter is necessary before one can know both, he who is ever to know a syllable must certainly know the letters first, and so our fine theory will have run away and vanished!

greek_head: Καὶ μάλα γε ἐξαίφνης Οὐ γὰρ καλῶς αὐτὸν φυλάττομεν χρῆν γὰρ ἴσως
greek_tokens: kai mala ge ecaifnhs ou gar kalos autos fulassw xraw gar isos o sullabh tiqhmi mh o stoixeion alla ek ekeinos eis tis gignomai eidos idea eis autos eautou exw eteros de o stoixeion panu men oun kai taxa ge an mallon outws h ekeinos exw skepteos kai ou prodoteon outws anandros megas te kai semnon logos ou gar oun
english_head: And very suddenly, too. Yes, for we are not watching it carefully. Perhaps we ought to have said that the syllable is not the letters, but a single concept that has arisen from them, having a single form of its own, different from the letters. Certainly; and perhaps that will be better than the other way. Let us look into that; we must not give up in such unmanly fashion a great and impressive theory. No, we must not.

greek_head: Ἐχέτω δὴ ὡς νῦν φαμεν μία ἰδέα ἐξ ἑκάστων τῶν συναρμοττόντων στοιχείων γιγνομένη ἡ συλλαβή ὁμοίως
greek_tokens: exw dh ws nun fhmi eis idea ek ekastos o sunarmozw stoixeion gignomai o sullabh omoios en te gramma kai en o allos apas panu men oun oukoun mera autos ou deomai eimi tis dh ostis e an eimi mera o oloc anagkh o pas mera eimi h kai o oloc ek o mera legw gignomai eis tis eidos eteros o pas mera egw o de dh pas kai o oloc poteros tautos kalew h
english_head: Let it be, then, as we say now, that the syllable or combination is a single form arising out of the several conjoined elements, and that it is the same in words and in all other things. Certainly. Therefore there must be no parts of it. How so? Because if there are parts of anything, the whole must inevitably be all the parts; or do you assert also that the whole that has arisen out of the parts is a single concept different from all the parts? Yes, I do. Do you then say that all and the whole

greek_head: ἕτερον ἑκάτερον Ἔχω μὲν οὐδὲν σαφές ὅτι δὲ κελεύεις προθύμως
greek_tokens: eteros ekateros exw men oudeis safhs ostis de keleuw proqumos apokrinw parakinduneuw legos ostis eteros o men proqumia eimi qeaithtos orqos ei de kai o apokrisls skepteos deomai ge dh oukoun diaferw an o oloc o pas ws o nun logos nai tis de dh o pas kai o pas esqw ostis diaferw oios epeidan legw eis duo treis tessares pente ec kai
english_head: or that each of the two is different from the other? I am not sure; but you tell me to answer boldly, so I take the risk and say that they are different. Your boldness, Theaetetus, is right; but whether your answer is so remains to be seen. Yes, certainly, we must see about that. The whole, then, according to our present view, would differ from all? Yes. How about this? Is there any difference between all in the plural and all in the singular? For instance, if we say one, two, three,

greek_head: ἐὰν δὶς τρία ἢ τρὶς δύο ἢ τέτταρά τε καὶ δύο ἢ τρία καὶ δύο καὶ ἕν πότερον ἐν πᾶσι τούτοις τὸ αὐτὸ ἢ ἕτερον λέγομεν
greek_tokens: ean dis treis h tris duo h tessares te kai duo h treis kai duo kai eis poteros en pas outos o autos h eteros legw o autos ara allos tis h ec oudeis oukoun epi ekastos lecis pas ec erw nai pas de oudeis legw o pas legw anagkh eimi allos tis h o ec oudeis
english_head: four, five, six, or twice three, or three times two, or four and two, or three and two and one, are we in all these forms speaking of the same or of different numbers? Of the same. That is, of six? Yes. Then in each form of speech we have spoken of all the six? Yes. And again do we not speak of one thing when we speak of them all? Assuredly. That is, of six? Yes.

greek_head: Ταὐτὸν ἄρα ἔν γε τοῖς ὅσα ἐξ ἀριθμοῦ ἐστι τό τε πᾶν προσαγορεύομεν καὶ τὰ ἅπαντα
greek_tokens: tautos ara en ge o osos ek ariqmew eimi o te pas prosagoreuw kai o apas fainw wde dh peri autos legw o o pleqron ariqmos kai o pleqron tautos eimi gar nai kai o o stadion dh wsautws nai kai meis kai o o stratopedon ge kai o stratopedon kai pas o toioutos omoios o gar ariqmos pas o eimi pas ekastos autos eimi nai
english_head: Then in all things that are made up of number, we apply the same term to all in the plural and all in the singular? Apparently. Here is another way of approaching the matter. The number of the fathom and the fathom are the same, are they not? Yes. And of the furlong likewise. Yes. And the number of the army is the same as the army, and all such cases are alike? In each of them all the number is all the thing. Yes. And is the number of each anything but

greek_head: Ὁ δὲ ἑκάστων ἀριθμὸς μῶν ἄλλο τι ἢ μέρη ἐστίν Οὐδέν
greek_tokens: o de ekastos ariqmos mhs allos tis h mera eimi oudeis osos ara exis mera ek mera an eaw fainw o de ge pas mera o pas eimi omologew eiper kai o pas ariqmos o pas eimi outws o oloc ara ou eimi ek mera pas gar an eaw o pas eimi mera ou eoika meros de esqw ostis allos eimi osper eimi h o oloc o pas ge
english_head: the parts of each? No. Everything that has parts, accordingly, consists of parts, does it not? Evidently. But we are agreed that the all must be all the parts if all the number is to be the all. Yes. Then the whole does not consist of parts, for if it consisted of all the parts it would be the all. That seems to be true. But is a part a part of anything in the world but the whole? Yes, of the all.

greek_head: Ἀνδρικῶς γε ὦ Θεαίτητε μάχῃ τὸ πᾶν δὲ οὐχ ὅταν μηδὲν ἀπῇ αὐτὸ τοῦτο πᾶν ἐστιν
greek_tokens: andrikos ge eimi qeaithtos maxh o pas de ou otan mhdeis apeimi autos outos pas eimi anagkh oloc de ou tautos outos eimi e an mhdamh mhdeis apostatew e de an apostatew oute oloc oute pas ama gignomai ek o autos o autos dokew egw nun oudeis diaferw pas te kai oloc oukoun legw ostis e an mera eimi o oloc te kai pas o pas mera eimi panu ge palin dh osper arti epixeirew ou eiper o
english_head: You are putting up a brave fight, Theaetetus. But is not the all precisely that of which nothing is wanting? Necessarily. And is not just this same thing, from which nothing whatsoever is lacking, a whole? For that from which anything is lacking is neither a whole nor all, which have become identical simultaneously and for the same reason. I think now that there is no difference between all and whole. We were saying, were we not, that if there are parts of anything, the whole and all of it will

english_head: that it contains the letters, not as parts of it, or else that being the same as the letters, it is equally knowable with them? It does. And it was in order to avoid this that we assumed that it was different from them? Yes. Well then, if the letters are not parts of the syllable, can you mention any other things which are parts of it, but are not the letters of it? Certainly not. For if I grant that there are parts of the syllable, it would be ridiculous to give up the letters and look for othe

greek_head: Παντάπασι δή ὦ Θεαίτητε κατὰ τὸν νῦν λόγον μία τις ἰδέα ἀμέριστος συλλαβὴ ἂν εἴη
greek_tokens: pantapasi dh eimi qeaithtos kata o nun logos eis tis idea ameristos sullabh an eaw eoika mimnhskw oun eimi filos ostis oligos en o prosqen apodexomai hgeomai eu legw ostis o proteros ou eaw logos ek os allos sugkeimai dioti autos kaqa eautou ekastos eaw asunqetos kai oude o eimi peri autos orqos exw prosferw eipon oude outos ws eteros kai allotrios legw kai outos dh o aitios alogos te kai agnwstos autos poiew mimnhskw
english_head: Without question, then, Theaetetus, the syllable would be, according to our present view, some indivisible concept. I agree. Do you remember, then, my friend, that we admitted a little while ago, on what we considered good grounds, that there can be no rational explanation of the primary elements of which other things are composed, because each of them, when taken by itself, is not composite, and we could not properly apply to such an element even the expression be or this, because these terms a

greek_head: Ἦ οὖν ἄλλη τις ἢ αὕτη ἡ αἰτία τοῦ μονοειδές τε καὶ ἀμέριστον αὐτὸ εἶναι ἐγὼ μὲν γὰρ οὐχ ὁρῶ ἄλλην
greek_tokens: eimi oun allos tis h outos o aitios o monoeidhs te kai ameristos autos eimi egw men gar ou oraw allos ou gar oun dh fainw oukoun eis tautos empitnw o sullabh eidos ekeinos eiper mera te mh exis kai eis eimi idea pantapasi men oun ei men ara polus stoixeion o sullabh eimi kai oloc tis mera de autos outos omoios o te sullabh gnwstos kai rhtos kai o stoixeion epei o pas mera o oloc tautos fainw
english_head: And is not this the sole reason why it is single in form and indivisible? I can see no other. There is no other to be seen. Then the syllable falls into the same class with the letter, if it has no parts and is a single form? Yes, unquestionably. If, then, the syllable is a plurality of letters and is a whole of which the letters are parts, the syllables and the letters are equally knowable and expressible, if all the parts were found to be the same as the whole.

greek_head: Καὶ μάλα Εἰ δέ γε ἕν τε καὶ ἀμερές ὁμοίως μὲν συλλαβή
greek_tokens: kai mala ei de ge eis te kai amerhs omoios men sullabh wsautws de stoixeion alogos te kai agnwstos o gar autos aitios poihsis autos toioutos ou exw allos eipon outos men ara mh apodexomai os an legos sullabh men gnwstos kai rhtos stoixeion de enantion mh gar eiper o logos peiqw
english_head: Certainly. But if one and indivisible, then syllable and likewise letter are equally irrational and unknowable; for the same cause will make them so. I cannot dispute it. Then we must not accept the statement of any one who says that the syllable is knowable and expressible, but the letter is not. No, not if we are convinced by our argument.

greek_head: Τί δ' αὖ τοὐναντίον λέγοντος ἆρ' οὐ μᾶλλον ἂν ἀποδέξαιο ἐξ ὧν αὐτὸς σύνοισθα σαυτῷ ἐν τῇ τῶν γραμμάτων
greek_tokens: tis de au enantion legw ara ou mallon an apodexomai ek os autos sunoida sautou en o o gramma maqhsis o poios ws oudeis allos manqanw diatelew h o stoixeion en te o oraw diagignwskw peiraw kai en o akoh autos kaqa eautou ekastos ina mh o qesis su tarassw legw te kai grafw alhqhs legw en de kiqarisths teleios manqanw mhs allos tis
english_head: But would you not rather accept the opposite belief, judging by your own experience when you were learning to read? What experience? In learning, you were merely constantly trying to distinguish between the letters both by sight and by hearing, keeping each of them distinct from the rest, that you might not be disturbed by their sequence when they were spoken or written. That is very true. And in the music school was not perfect attainment

greek_head: ἦν ἢ τὸ τῷ φθόγγῳ ἑκάστῳ δύνασθαι ἐπακολουθεῖν ποίας χορδῆς εἴη ἃ δὴ στοιχεῖα πᾶς ἂν ὁμολογήσειε μουσικῆς
greek_tokens: eimi h o o fqoggos ekastos dunamai epakolouqew poa xordh eaw o dh stoixeion pas an omologew mousikh legw oudeis allos os men ara autos empeiros eimi stoixeion kai sullabh ei deomai apo outos tekmairomai kai eis o allos polus o o stoixeion genos enarghs te o gignwskw exw fhmi kai kurios o sullabh pros o lambanw teleios ekastos maqhma kai ean tis fhmi sullabh men gnwstos agnwstos de fuw stoixeion ekwn h akwn paizw ageomai autos komidh men oun
english_head: the ability to follow each note and tell which string produced it; and everyone would agree that the notes are the elements of music? Yes, that is all true. Then if we are to argue from the elements and combinations in which we ourselves have experience to other things in general, we shall say that the elements as a class admit of a much clearer knowledge than the compounds and of a knowledge that is much more important for the complete attainment of each branch of learning, and if anyone says t

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ δὴ τούτου μὲν ἔτι κἂν ἄλλαι φανεῖεν ἀποδείξεις ὡς ἐμοὶ δοκεῖ τὸ δὲ προκείμενον μὴ ἐπιλαθώμεθα

english_head: Still other proofs of this might be brought out, I think; but let us not on that account lose sight of the question before us, which is: What is meant by the doctrine that the most perfect knowledge arises from the addition of rational explanation to true opinion? No, we must not. Now what are we intended to understand by rational explanation? I think it means one of three things. What are they?

greek_head: Τὸ μὲν πρῶτον εἴη ἂν τὸ τὴν αὑτοῦ διάνοιαν ἐμφανῆ ποιεῖν διὰ φωνῆς μετὰ ῥημάτων τε καὶ ὀνομάτων
greek_tokens: o men proteros eaw an o o eautou dianoia emfanhs poiew dia fwnew meta rhma te kai onoma wsper eis katoptron h udwr o doca ektupow eis o dia o stoma roh h ou dokew su o toioutos logos eimi egw o goun autos draw legw fhmi oukoun au outos ge pas poiew dunatos qasswn h sxolaios o endeiknumi tis dokew peri ekastos autos o mh eneos h kwfos apo arxh kai outws osos tis orqos
english_head: The first would be making one’s own thought clear through speech by means of verbs and nouns, imaging the opinion in the stream that flows through the lips, as in a mirror or water. Do you not think the rational explanation is something of that sort? Yes, I do. At any rate, we say that he who does that speaks or explains. Well, that is a thing that anyone can do sooner or later; he can show what he thinks about anything, unless he is deaf or dumb from the first; and so

greek_head: δοξάζουσι πάντες αὐτὸ μετὰ λόγου φανοῦνται ἔχοντες καὶ οὐδαμοῦ ἔτι ὀρθὴ δόξα χωρὶς ἐπιστήμης γενήσεται
greek_tokens: docazw pas autos meta logos fainw exw kai oudamos eti orqos doca xwris episthmh gignomai alhqhs mh toinun radios katagignwskw o mhdeis erw o apofainw episthmh o nun skopaw isos gar o legos ou outos legw alla o erwtaw tis ekastos dunatos eimi o apokrisls dia o stoixeion
english_head: all who have any right opinion will be found to have it with the addition of rational explanation, and there will henceforth be no possibility of right opinion apart from knowledge. True. Let us not, therefore, carelessly accuse him of talking nonsense who gave the definition of knowledge which we are now considering; for perhaps that is not what he meant. He may have meant that each person if asked about anything must be able in reply

Now I could not name the pieces, nor, I fancy, could you; but if we were asked what a wagon is, we should be satisfied if we could say wheels, axle, body, rims, yoke. Certainly. But he, perhaps, would think we were ridiculous, just as he would if, on being asked about your name, we should reply by telling the syllable

Do you agree to that, Socrates? If you, my friend, agree to it and accept the view that orderly description in terms of its elements is a rational account of anything, but that description in terms of syllables or still larger units is irrational,

english_head: and sometimes another belonged to the same syllable, and when we put the same letter sometimes into the proper syllable and sometimes into another? That is what I mean. By Zeus, I do not forget, nor do I think that those have knowledge who are in that condition. Take an example: When at such a stage in his progress a person in writing Theaetetus thinks he ought to write,

english_head: and actually does write, TH and E, and again in trying to write Theodorus thinks he ought to write, and does write, T and E, shall we say that he knows the first syllable of your names? No, we just now agreed that a person in such a condition has not yet gained knowledge. Then there is nothing to prevent the same person from being in that condition with respect to the second and third and fourth syllables? No, nothing. Then, in that case, he has in mind the orderly description in terms of letter

english_head: But he is still, as we say, without knowledge, though he has right opinion? Yes. Yes, but with his opinion he has rational explanation; for he wrote with the method in terms of letters in his mind, and we agreed that that was rational explanation. True. There is, then, my friend, a combination of right opinion with rational explanation, which cannot as yet properly be called knowledge? There is not much doubt about it. So it seems that the perfectly true definition of knowledge, which we thought

english_head: but the remaining one of the three possibilities one of which we said must be affirmed by anyone who asserts that knowledge is right opinion combined with rational explanation. I am glad you called that to mind. For there is still one left. The first was a kind of vocal image of the thought, the second the orderly approach to the whole through the elements, which we have just been discussing, and what is the third? english_head: As an example, if you like, take the sun: I think it is enough for you to be told that it is the brightest of the heavenly bodies that revolve about the earth. Certainly. Understand why I say this. It is because, as we were just saying, if you get hold of the distinguishing characteristic by which a given thing differs from the rest, you will, as some say, get hold of the definition or explanation of it; but so long as you cling to some common quality, your explanation will pertain to all those

english_head: I understand; and it seems to me that it is quite right to call that kind a rational explanation or definition. Then he who possesses right opinion about anything and adds thereto a comprehension of the difference which distinguishes it from other things will have acquired knowledge of that thing of which he previously had only opinion. english_head: I will tell you if I can. Assume that I have right opinion about you; if I add the explanation or definition of you, then I have knowledge of you, otherwise I have merely opinion. Yes. But explanation was, we agreed, the interpretation of your difference. It was. Then so long as I had merely opinion, I did not grasp in my thought any of the points in which you differ from others? Apparently not. Therefore I was thinking of some one of the common traits which you possess no more than other men.

english_head: but of one with snub nose and protruding eyes, shall I then have an opinion of you any more than of myself and all others like me? Not at all. No; I fancy Theaetetus will not be the object of opinion in me until this snubnosedness of yours has stamped and deposited in my mind a memorial different from those of the other examples of snubnosedness that I have seen, and the other traits that make up your personality have done the like. english_head: Then right opinion also would have to do with differences in any given instance? At any rate, it seems so. Then what becomes of the addition of reason or explanation to right opinion? For if it is defined as the addition of an opinion of the way in which a given thing differs from the rest, it is an utterly absurd injunction. How so? When we have a right opinion of the way in which certain things differ from other things, we are told to acquire a right opinion of the way in which those same thin

english_head: compared with this injunction. It might more justly be called a blind man’s giving directions; for to command us to acquire that which we already have, in order to learn that of which we already have opinion, is very like a man whose sight is mightily darkened. Tell me now, what did you intend to say when you asked the question a while ago? If, my boy, the command to add reason or explanation means learning to know and not merely getting an opinion about the difference, our splendid definition o

english_head: is it not? Yes. Then, it seems, if asked, What is knowledge? our leader will reply that it is right opinion with the addition of a knowledge of difference; for that would, according to him, be the addition of reason or explanation. So it seems. And it is utterly silly, when we are looking for a definition of knowledge, to say that it is right opinion with knowledge, whether of difference or of anything else whatsoever. So neither perception, Theaetetus, nor true opinion, nor reason or explanatio

english_head: combined with true opinion could be knowledge. Apparently not. Are we then, my friend, still pregnant and in travail with knowledge, or have we brought forth everything? Yes, we have, and, by Zeus, Socrates, with your help I have already said more than there was in me. Then does our art of midwifery declare to us that all the offspring that have been born are mere wind-eggs and not worth rearing? It does, decidedly. english_head: you will be pregnant with better thoughts than these by reason of the present search, and if you remain barren, you will be less harsh and gentler to your associates, for you will have the wisdom not to think you know that which you do not know. So much and no more my art can accomplish; nor do I know aught of the things that are known by others, the great and wonderful men who are today and have been in the past. english_head: And now I must go to the Porch of the King, to answer to the suit which Meletus has brought against me. But in the morning, Theodorus, let us meet here again.
